name: Immutability
description: The philosophy that data cannot be modified after its creation, eliminating shared mutable state bugs.

name: The Actor Model
description: A model of concurrent computation where isolated entities communicate exclusively via message passing.

name: CSP - Communicating Sequential Processes
description: A formal language for describing patterns of interaction in concurrent systems via channels.

name: Async/Await
description: A syntactic feature that allows asynchronous code to be written in a structured, synchronous-like style.

name: Hindley-Milner Type System
description: A formal type system that provides automatic type inference for strictly typed languages.

name: Garbage Collection
description: Automatic memory management that identifies and reclaims memory no longer in use by the program.

name: RAII - Resource Acquisition Is Initialization
description: A programming idiom where resource lifecycle is tied to object scope and destructors.

name: Ownership & Borrowing
description: A memory management system that uses compile-time rules to ensure safety without a garbage collector.

name: Macros
description: A system for defining code transformations that are expanded by the compiler or preprocessor.

name: Homoiconicity
description: A property where the program's structure is itself a data structure of the language.

name: The Software Crisis
description: The historical realization that software complexity often outpaces our tools and methodologies.

name: Semantic Search
description: Information retrieval that focuses on the conceptual intent of a query rather than literal keywords.

name: Borrow Checking
description: The compile-time analysis used to enforce ownership and reference validity rules.

name: Block structure
description: A lexical scoping mechanism where code is organized into nested blocks with their own scope.

name: Design by Contract
description: A formal approach to software design using preconditions, postconditions, and invariants.

name: Event Loop
description: A programming construct that waits for and dispatches events or messages in a program.

name: Multiple Inheritance
description: A feature where a class can inherit behaviors and features from more than one parent class.

name: Nominal vs Structural Typing
description: The distinction between typing based on explicit names versus typing based on data shape.

name: Object-Oriented Design
description: A design philosophy centered on objects, encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism.

name: Relational Model
description: A model for database management based on first-order predicate logic and set theory.

name: Static vs Dynamic Typing
description: The fundamental divide between compile-time and runtime type checking.

name: Church-Turing Thesis
description: The hypothesis that every effectively calculable function is computable by a Turing machine.

name: Algebraic Data Types
description: Composing data from sums and products so programs can model closed sets of cases precisely.

name: Type Inference
description: Automatically deducing program types from how values are defined and used.

name: Type Classes
description: A disciplined form of ad-hoc polymorphism that attaches behavior to families of types through explicit constraints.

name: Structural Typing
description: Type compatibility based on shape and available operations rather than declared identity.

name: Nominal Typing
description: Type compatibility based on explicit declarations, names, and lineage rather than object shape alone.

name: Gradual Typing
description: Combining dynamic and static typing so codebases can move between looser and stricter checking over time.

name: Module System
description: A language-level mechanism for organizing code into named units with explicit interfaces and composition rules.

name: Functors
description: Parameterized modules in the ML tradition that compute new modules from other modules.

name: Pattern Matching
description: Destructuring and branching on data shape directly in the language, often alongside algebraic types.

name: Unification
description: Solving symbolic constraints by finding substitutions that make expressions structurally consistent.

name: Array-First Programming
description: A style of computing that treats whole arrays as the primary semantic unit, making data-parallel transformation more central than scalar control flow.

name: Arrays as First-Class Citizens
description: Designing a language so arrays are native values with rich operators, not just containers wrapped around scalar code.

name: Broadcasting
description: Generalized scalar-shape interaction that lets array expressions combine values of different shapes without writing explicit replication loops.

name: Arbitrary-Precision Arithmetic
description: Computing with exact or expansible numeric precision so symbolic systems and technical tools are not forced into fixed-width approximations.

name: Algebraic Expression Evaluation
description: Treating formulas as executable symbolic structures that can be simplified, transformed, differentiated, and solved inside the programming environment.

name: Notebook Computing
description: An interactive document-computation model where code, prose, formulas, graphics, and results live together in one technical workspace.

name: Symbolic Pattern Recognition
description: Pattern-directed matching over symbolic expressions so rewriting systems can recognize algebraic structure instead of only concrete values.

name: Interactive Numerical Exploration
description: Using a command-driven numerical environment to iterate on models, data, and visual output in immediate conversation with the machine.

name: Integrated Plotting and Analysis
description: A technical-computing workflow where data analysis, plotting, and iterative model refinement happen inside the same language environment.

name: ASCII-Based Array Syntax
description: Expressing array-language ideas with portable ASCII notation instead of specialized glyph sets, preserving array semantics in text-first environments.
